title: [PLACEHOLDER] Delegated act on evaluation methodology for general-purpose AI models under the AI Act
summary: Specifies the technical standards, benchmarks and protocols that providers of general-purpose AI models must follow when carrying out capability and systemic-risk evaluations required by Article 55 of the AI Act, including red-teaming requirements and reporting templates.
rationale: This delegated act sets binding evaluation methodology for general-purpose AI models under Article 55 of the AI Act — a foundational technical rule for frontier-AI safety governance with long-term lock-in. Safety-focused technical input at this stage is disproportionately influential relative to the heavy industry response expected. The combination of x-risk relevance, durability, and an open technical design space makes it a high-leverage target for EA-aligned experts.

scope.note: Governs evaluation of all GPAI models placed on the EU market and will likely anchor evaluation practice globally given the Brussels effect.

reversibility.note: Delegated acts are formally revisable but the methodology, benchmarks, and reporting templates set here will entrench industry practice for many years.

tractability.note: Mid-stage consultation on technical implementation — high-level direction is fixed by the AI Act but specific protocols, thresholds, and red-teaming requirements remain genuinely open.

urgency.note: Deadline 2026-06-15 is roughly 6+ months out, leaving ample time but no immediate pressure to respond.

counterfactual.note: Industry and major labs will submit heavily; independent AI safety and evaluation-focused technical voices remain comparatively under-represented.

title: [PLACEHOLDER] Revision of Council Directive 1999/74/EC on minimum standards for the protection of laying hens
summary: Reviews cage-size minimums, enrichment requirements, stocking densities and the timeline for phasing out enriched cages across all EU member states. Considers extending welfare requirements to imported eggs.
rationale: This is a foundational revision of the EU's core laying-hen welfare directive, directly affecting hundreds of millions of hens annually and potentially extending welfare standards to imports. Animal welfare consultations on cage phase-outs are exactly where EA-aligned input has historically had outsized influence, and industry counter-lobbying is intense. The deadline is comfortably distant but the policy stakes are high.

scope.note: Affects ~370 million laying hens in the EU annually plus potential spillover to import-supplying countries via the import provision.

reversibility.note: Directive revisions of this kind typically lock in for 10-20 years given the capital cycle of cage infrastructure.

tractability.note: Revision is genuinely open: cage phase-out timeline, stocking densities, and the novel import-equivalence question are all live design choices.

urgency.note: Deadline ~6 months away, leaving ample time but no immediate pressure to respond.

counterfactual.note: Major animal welfare NGOs (CIWF, Eurogroup) will engage heavily, but industry submissions typically outnumber welfare voices and additional expert input on import-equivalence design is valuable.

title: [PLACEHOLDER] Implementing regulation on transparency obligations for deployers of high-risk AI systems
summary: Sets the format, content and frequency of transparency reports that deployers of high-risk AI systems must publish, including model cards, evaluation results and incident logs. Open consultation; final rules expected late 2026.
rationale: This implementing regulation defines the transparency reporting regime for deployers of high-risk AI systems under the AI Act — a sectoral but cross-cutting governance rule covering many high-stakes domains. It is squarely AI governance; it does not substantively address frontier/GPAI safety or x-risk, so we tag only ai_governance. Good independent technical input on disclosure formats can meaningfully improve the regime.

scope.note: Transparency rules will apply to all deployers of high-risk AI across the EU, shaping accountability in employment, education, biometrics, critical infrastructure and more.

reversibility.note: Implementing acts can be revised, but reporting templates and conventions tend to entrench industry practice for years.

tractability.note: Open consultation framed as still-formative on format, content and frequency — concrete drafting suggestions can land.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 4-6 months out (relative to the late-2026 finalisation), giving ample time without immediate pressure.

counterfactual.note: Deployer industry and large vendors will dominate submissions; civil-society and safety-oriented voices on disclosure design are typically under-represented.

title: [PLACEHOLDER] Review of Council Regulation (EC) No 1/2005 on the protection of animals during transport
summary: Considers maximum journey times, temperature thresholds, ventilation standards and whether long-distance live exports outside the EU should be phased out. Draws on the EFSA scientific opinion published in 2023.
rationale: This is a major revision of the EU's core animal transport regulation, directly addressing journey times, conditions, and the contentious question of long-distance live exports — affecting hundreds of millions of farmed animals annually. Animal welfare advocates have substantial but not overwhelming presence on transport policy, and specific technical provisions remain genuinely open at this stage of the review.

scope.note: The regulation governs transport of hundreds of millions of farmed animals per year across the EU and to third countries, with deep welfare implications per animal.

reversibility.note: A foundational sectoral regulation that, once revised, will anchor transport practice for many years before another full review.

tractability.note: Core questions like live export phase-out and journey-time caps are genuinely contested and open to influence at this review stage.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 6 months out, leaving ample time but no immediate pressure to act.

counterfactual.note: Established animal welfare NGOs (Eurogroup for Animals, CIWF) will respond strongly, but additional thoughtful submissions — especially with EA-aligned welfare-range reasoning — still add marginal value against heavy industry pushback.

title: [PLACEHOLDER] Update of the EU eIDAS regulation on electronic identification and trust services
summary: Refines technical standards for the European Digital Identity Wallet, including cross-border interoperability and revocation mechanisms. Targeted at digital identity providers and member-state authorities.
rationale: This consultation concerns technical standards for the EU Digital Identity Wallet under eIDAS — a digital infrastructure / single-market topic without substantive overlap with EA cause areas. While digital identity has implications for privacy and civil liberties, it does not map onto AI governance, x-risk, animal welfare, or other priority areas in the taxonomy.

scope.note: Affects digital identity infrastructure used by hundreds of millions of EU citizens, but the welfare-relevant impact is diffuse.

reversibility.note: Technical standards for foundational digital ID infrastructure carry inertia but can be amended through normal regulatory updates.

tractability.note: Late-stage refinement of technical standards where the overall framework and direction are already set.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 5-6 months out, providing ample time but no near-term pressure.

counterfactual.note: Digital identity providers, civil liberties NGOs, and member-state authorities will respond heavily; marginal value of an EA-aligned submission is low.

title: [PLACEHOLDER] Strengthening the EU pandemic preparedness framework — call for evidence on engineered pathogen risk
summary: Assesses the EU's preparedness for engineered pandemic pathogens, including DNA synthesis screening, lab biosafety oversight, dual-use research-of-concern policy, and the role of the Health Emergency Preparedness and Response Authority (HERA). Aims to inform a 2027 legislative proposal.
rationale: This call for evidence directly addresses engineered pandemic pathogen risk — a core EA biosecurity priority and a recognized x-risk driver — covering DNA synthesis screening, dual-use research oversight, and lab biosafety. As an early-stage consultation feeding a 2027 legislative proposal, it is unusually open to shaping framing and is likely under-attended by biosecurity-focused voices. The decisions will set foundational EU biosecurity architecture with long-horizon implications.

scope.note: Engineered pandemic governance has civilization-scale stakes and the resulting EU framework would influence global biosecurity norms (e.g., synthesis screening standards).

reversibility.note: A 2027 legislative proposal anchored on this evidence base would lock in EU biosecurity architecture for a decade or more.

tractability.note: Early-stage call for evidence informing a future legislative proposal — framing and scope of the eventual instrument are still genuinely movable.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 10-12 weeks out (assuming current date in early 2026), giving a comfortable window for a substantive response.

counterfactual.note: Biosecurity expertise on engineered-pathogen policy is thin in EU consultation processes; industry and generalist public-health actors will dominate without targeted EA-aligned input.

title: [PLACEHOLDER] Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) — review of sectoral coverage and default values
summary: Reviews the scope of CBAM, including potential extension to chemicals and polymers, the methodology for default emission values for non-reporting importers, and interaction with the EU ETS phase-out of free allowances.
rationale: This consultation reviews CBAM's sectoral scope and methodology, a core EU climate policy instrument that affects emissions from imports into the single market. While substantively important for climate mitigation, it is heavily lobbied by industry and well-covered by mainstream climate NGOs, lowering the marginal value of an EA-aligned submission. The technical framing (default values, chemicals/polymers extension) is open but mid-stage.

scope.note: CBAM shapes emissions accounting for major imported industrial sectors into the EU and influences global carbon pricing norms.

reversibility.note: Methodology and scope decisions create substantial inertia for industry compliance, though revisable through future reviews.

tractability.note: Mid-stage review with specific provisions (chemicals/polymers extension, default values) genuinely open to input.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 4-6 months away, allowing ample preparation time without immediate pressure.

counterfactual.note: Saturated topic — mainstream climate NGOs, think tanks, and industry will respond extensively regardless.

title: [PLACEHOLDER] Programming of the Global Gateway and EU development cooperation 2028-2034
summary: Sets the priorities and indicative budget envelopes for EU development cooperation, including allocations across global health (malaria, TB, HIV, maternal health), humanitarian assistance, education, and infrastructure investment in low- and lower-middle-income countries.
rationale: This consultation shapes the EU's development cooperation priorities and budget allocations for 2028-2034, directly affecting funding for high-impact global health interventions like malaria, TB, and maternal health in low-income countries. As a foundational seven-year programming cycle with multi-decade implications for global poverty reduction, it warrants the longtermism secondary tag. EA-aligned voices advocating for evidence-based, cost-effective health interventions are typically underrepresented relative to sectoral lobbies in development programming.

scope.note: A seven-year EU development envelope affects hundreds of millions of people in low- and lower-middle-income countries through health, humanitarian, and infrastructure funding.

reversibility.note: Multi-year programming framework locks in priorities and budget envelopes for 2028-2034 with substantial inertia once adopted.

tractability.note: Early-stage programming consultation where priority-setting and allocation framings are still genuinely movable.

urgency.note: Deadline appears to be roughly 6+ months out, providing a comfortable window to prepare a substantive submission.

counterfactual.note: Development programming consultations are typically dominated by sectoral NGOs and industry; EA-aligned voices pushing cost-effectiveness and GiveWell-style priorities are rare.
